posts.cooked:
Took this as a mystery to BacchusBadBoys last night:

  1. PaulJaboulet LaChapelle AC: Hermitage (13%) Tain l’Hermitage 1989: Med.dark color w/ some bricking; somewhat smokey/Rhonish/roasted/coffee slight bacon fat/black olive very slight floral/violets/Syrah/spicy rather complex nose; lightly tart slight tangy/metallic very smooth/gentle/quiet some Rhonish/smokey/coffee/roasted bit bacony light floral/Syrah fairly complex flavor w/ light smooth/gentle tannins; very long smooth/gentle some smokey/Rhonish/coffee/roasted slight tangy/metallic complex finish w/ light gentle tannins; in much better shape than I expected; speaks of fully mature NRhone w/ a quiet voice; lovely elegant/delicate wine that’s gently fading away into the sunset. $40.00

A wee BloodyPulpit:

  1. Most of the tasters identified it as an older Syrah but thinking Calif. Which is understandable as I think Hermitage is the most Calif in style of the NRhone. None guessed it as Hermitage and were rather surprised to find it high-end Hermitage. I was expecting a bit more “oomph” to the wine. It’s a quiet little old lady in the twilight of her days, fading quietly into the sunset, but still w/ a few risque stories to whisper in your ear.
    Tom

posts.name: Bruce Leiser_owitz
posts.cooked:
Provenance/storage?

Bruce

posts.name: Markus S
posts.cooked: “Fading” sounds a bit harsh. Not sure when I last had this, but sometime in the late 2000’s and it was youthful as bananas; I would have said to try again in another 10 (probably about now) and have it go for another 20.

Bruce Leiser_owitz:

Provenance/storage?

Bruce

Bruce,
Not from my orphan stash in the garage. This was stored in a stash in my son’s closet from the mid-'90’s, on its side.
Temps in the Summer into the upper 60’s. Running about upper 40’s right now in wintertime. Large thermal mass
so not much daily cycling.
Tom

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Does sound prematurely senescent compared to a bottle I had last year.

posts.name: Larry Archibald
posts.cooked:
My experience of Tom’s bottle was that is was not a lovely elegant wine fading into the sunset. Had a lot of fruit but a fully mature character. I think it, or bottles like it, would easily be good for another five years. Bottles stored at lower temperature are probably more vibrant and ready to go longer.

Compared to the bottles of '90 I’ve had, this shows a riper, more California-like character. I loved it, but didn’t think it was French. Didn’t seem to have any Northern Rhone character, and no other particularly French character I could detect.

I think Tom’s expectation of greater “oomph” might have kept him from enjoying its non-oomphy, but very present, charms.

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked: Does sound nice, but a little advanced. At that age, every bottle is unique. A recent bottle brought to an offline was quite good: nice dark garnet, gorgeous deep, dark, cherry, cassis, hints of leather, still youthful, smooth and elegant.

posts.name: Bruce Leiser_owitz
posts.cooked:
TomHill:
Bruce Leiser_owitz:

Provenance/storage?

Bruce

Bruce,
Not from my orphan stash in the garage. This was stored in a stash in my son’s closet from the mid-'90’s, on its side.
Temps in the Summer into the upper 60’s. Running about upper 40’s right now in wintertime. Large thermal mass
so not much daily cycling.
Tom

I would say that a non-temperature/non-humidity controlled closet isn’t optimal storage, especially when you’re talking about two decades of cellaring.

Bruce

posts.name: Glenn L e v i n e
posts.cooked: I agree with Alan & Bruce. Every 25 y/o wine bottle is unique and the storage described is not optimal.

Agree, “fading” doesn´t sound like a perfectly stored bottle, usually should be close to maturity now (even a bit youthful if stored very cool), but certainly not THAT mature …

Bruce Leiser_owitz:

I would say that a non-temperature/non-humidity controlled closet isn’t optimal storage, especially when you’re talking about two decades of cellaring.

Bruce

(my passive cellar is almost perfect, 46-54° F … 75-80 % hum)

posts.display_username: Gerhard P.  (P r @ € s e n t)

posts.cooked:
Bruce Leiser_owitz:
Gerhard P.:

Agree, “fading” doesn´t sound like a perfectly stored bottle, usually should be close to maturity now (even a bit youthful if stored very cool), but certainly not THAT mature …

Bruce Leiser_owitz:

I would say that a non-temperature/non-humidity controlled closet isn’t optimal storage, especially when you’re talking about two decades of cellaring.

Bruce

That´s vm depending WHERE it is and how are highest and lowest degrees temp. …
(my passive cellar is almost perfect, 46-54° F … 75-80 % hum)

I would say your passive cellar conditions are vastly superior to the closet that Tom Hill described, both in terms of temperature and humidity.

Bruce

Vastly superior, Bruce?? And you know this because of what evidence?? Perhaps you could characterize it as “incredibly superior”? Or “somewhat superior”? Or “marginally superior”?
I, or most, would assert that the “ideal” LaChapelle Hermitage '89 is one that has never moved from the Jaboulet cellars. Gerhard’sLaChapelle has “suffered” because it has “travelled”
from Tain l’Hermitage to Graz (under unknown shipping conditions), has been subjected to 10F fluctuations, has a humidity higher (or lower) than the Jaboulet cellars. So why would I trust at TN for
a LaChapelle from Gerhard’s cellar?? It is incredibly/vastly/somewhat/marginally (you choose) conditions from the Jaboulet-stored wine.
We all “know” that the ideal storage for a wine is a constant 55F, no temperature fluctuations, at 78.9 % humidity, stored on its side (not upright or upside down), covered by mold&dust.
This is stuff we all “know” because we’ve been told that by the wine experts that have preceeded us. We don’t dare to question what they “know” and have told us. End of discussion!!

I fully acknowledge that my wines are not stored under “optimal” conditions. Many of my wines are stored in my “cellar” in the garage. Temps from low 30F in the Winter, upper 60F in the
Summer, low humidity (this is, after all, NewMexico, drier than the Sahara Desert), some standing upright, some on their sides, some upside down. Mouse droppings on the floor that will,
of course, contaminate the wine. Air conditioner that unleashes dreadful vibrations on the wine in the Summer. Would the LaChapelle from my cellar have tasted better, less advanced, than one
from my son’s closet?? Incredibly/vastly/somewhat/marginally?? I have no idea and unable to perform that experiment, obviously.
The Vernay '85, stored in the same box, side-by-side w/ the Jaboulet, was a terrific example of a fully mature Cote-Rotie. Would a btl from my “cellar” have been better? From the Vernay cellar been
better? Incredibly/vastly/somewhat/marginally better? I haven’t a clue. And what is “better”? More fruit? More tannin? Younger seeming?? Beats heck out of me.
The “orphan stash” in the garage (now wiped out)?? Some of those German and Alsatian whites were amazingly good. They should have been DOA. This is something I “know”.
Sub-optimal storage for almost 20 yrs. They were not. Better than the same btls from the JJPrum or the Weinbach cellars? Probably not, but you never know.

I happen to believe that wine is a much more robust beverage than what we “know” to be true. I always get amused when folks freak out because UPS/FedEx delivered their wine in
80F temperatures (not to mention 30% or 90% humidity) and send it back to the wnry. Of course, I would prefer to store all my wine at “ideal” conditions (constant 55F, 78.9% humidity, no
vibrations, no mouse droppings on the floor). Alas, I have a real life and not all the time in the World to get stressed by those things.
So you should ignore any TN’s I put up because the wine has not been stored at “ideal” conditions. All those Idlewild Whites I just reviewed were stored standing upright, at uncontrolled temperatures,
43.7% humidity. The ones from Sam’s cellar would undoubtedly have been better. Incredibly/vastly/somewhat/marginally better? Haven’t a clue!!
Friday should be interesting. I thoroughly trashed Matt’s F-H QueSaudade '15 as being vapid/watery. The only good thing I could say about it was the beautiful fractals the CO2
formed on the surface of the wine. He’ll bring a btl from his cellar and I’ll have my other btl, stored at less-than “optimal” conditions; and we’ll taste them side-by-side. His btl will
be better (if you ignore the UnitedAirlines baggage handling), this is something I just “know”. Stay tuned.
Tom

posts.name: Gerhard P.  (P r @ € s e n t)
posts.cooked:
TomHill:

…

Vastly superior, Bruce?? And you know this because of what evidence?? Perhaps you could characterize it as “incredibly superior”? Or “somewhat superior”? Or “marginally superior”? …

I don´t participate in this “superior” discussion …

FWIW: I bought some La chapelles at Jaboulet shortly after release, and others (also at Jaboulet) several years later.
My impression is that the earlier purchased bottles are “better” … and IMHO not due to any storage conditions there or here, but due to (possible) earlier bottling …

In other words: Often there were several bottlings a Jaboulet, and the later bottlings are somewhat less impressive …

My impression is that the earlier purchased bottles are “better” … and IMHO not due to any storage conditions there or here, but due to (possible) earlier bottling …

In other words: Often there were several bottlings a Jaboulet, and the later bottlings are somewhat less impressive …

Smart move, Gerhard!!

My point is that whenever you taste a btl that old, the likehood of a different btl being the same is pretty small. Different provenance, different cork, all sorts of things.
If you “know” (or think you “know”) that a LaChapelle '89 should taste better than my btl, even if you just had a btl from the Jaboulet cellars,
it’s easy to blame it on less than “optimal” storage conditions. Maybe so/maybe not.
Tom

posts.name: Gerhard P.  (P r @ € s e n t)
posts.cooked:
TomHill:

My point is that whenever you taste a btl that old, the likehood of a different btl being the same is pretty small. Different provenance, different cork, all sorts of things. …

Yes, granted, but if bought by the case at the same time/same source the odds they are pretty similar are much higher … especially when you store it rather cool …

Generally I´ve experienced the biggest differences between bottles (same wine) when coming from different cellars (countries) - or if they were (most probably) different bottlings.

TomHill:

…
it’s easy to blame it on less than “optimal” storage conditions. Maybe so/maybe not.
Tom

FWIW:
A friend of mine once (app.2 years ago) served a Beaucastel 1998 blind which was VERY mature (almost slightly fading) - I didn´t recognize it, thought it was an older cdP of lesser reputation - I rated it 89 points.
Coincidently I had tasted ´98 Beau. two weeks earlier from my own cellar, and that was much fresher and stronger, close to maturity, but definitely not fading, easily worth 95 points.

My friend had bought his bottle in GB some 8-9 years ago, and his cellar is much warmer than mine.
I´ve bough mine in 2001 from the Austrian importer …

Tom–Given how many years you’ve been tasting wine, I’m sure you have a lot of familiarity with bottle variation in the same wine. Bottle variation can be due to a number of factors, including
variation in corks. Most of us have had the experience of buying multiple bottles of the same wine, storing them all the same, but then having some bottles show wonderfully while others are
really tired.

But one of the factors in bottle variation is storage/provenance. Looking at the 1990 La Chapelle, I’ve seen some tasting notes that indicate the wine is already “fully-mature;” other notes indicate the
wine is at best at the earliest stages of maturity. That was a wine I bought on release and put directly into a professional storage facility; all of the bottles I’ve opened have shown relatively young.

By contrast, I didn’t have access to a wine cabinet or professional storage when I first started collecting in the late 1970’s. The earliest bottles I collected way back then all show the effects of
long-term storage at room temperature.

Really, it’s not rocket science. Wines stored long term at higher than optimal temperatures, and/or at low humidity, tend to oxidize faster than those stored under optimal conditions. There’s someone
I know who has stored much of his collection under sub-optimal conditions, and his bottles tend to show more tired than comparable bottles stored in a professional wine facility.

Bruce

posts.cooked: I believe both the 89 and the 90 were cooked during the shipping, a lot of bottle variations like the 89 Beaucastel.
